sentences.text: What Does "cal" Mean?
The "cal" in cal/cm² stands for calorie — a unit of thermal (heat) energy.
In everyday life, you know calories as a food energy measurement, but in physics a calorie is simply the amount of heat required to raise one gram of water by one degree Celsius.
In arc flash safety, a calorie is used to measure how much heat energy reaches a worker's body during an arc flash event.
The full unit — cal/cm² — is "calories per square centimeter," meaning the amount of heat energy landing on each square centimeter of a person's body at a given distance from the arc.
The higher the number, the more heat per square centimeter, and the more severe the potential burn.
The key reference point: 1.2 cal/cm²: At 1.2 cal/cm², unprotected skin will sustain a second-degree burn — blistering, pain, and potential scarring.

This is not a catastrophic amount of energy in absolute terms: one useful mental image is holding your hand one inch over the flame of a cigarette lighter for one second, which delivers roughly 1 cal/cm².
The arc flash boundary on every label you'll see in an MCC room is calculated at exactly this threshold — the point where bare skin begins to be seriously injured.
How Incident Energy Is Calculated.

Incident energy — the number on your arc flash label in cal/cm² — is calculated by electrical engineers using IEEE Standard 1584, based on real arc flash tests across hundreds of equipment configurations.
Three factors drive the result more than anything else: Available fault current: How much current can flow through the arc.
Higher fault current means more energy — but not always more incident energy at the label, because circuit breakers clear faster at higher currents.
Arc duration (clearing time): How long the arc burns before the upstream breaker or fuse trips it off.
This is the single biggest driver of incident energy — time is more critical than current.
An arc lasting 0.5 seconds releases 10× more energy than one lasting 0.05 seconds.
Working distance: How far the worker's face and chest are from the arc.
Incident energy drops with the square of distance — double the distance, and the incident energy drops to roughly one quarter.
This is why your working distance matters and why standing to the side of an open bucket matters.

The result of that calculation is placed on the arc flash label on each piece of equipment, along with the arc flash boundary distance, the PPE category or minimum cal/cm² rating required, and the working distance the calculation assumed.
Always check the label — never assume.
Why time is the most dangerous variable: A 480V MCC bucket at a facility with slow-clearing breakers can easily reach 20–40 cal/cm² of incident energy.
The same physical bucket at a facility with fast arc flash relay protection might show only 2–5 cal/cm².
The voltage is the same.
The difference is entirely how long the breaker takes to clear.
This is why a label from a different facility or a generic "480V = low hazard" assumption can get someone killed — the incident energy must be calculated for that specific installation.
PPE Categories and What They Require.
The four NFPA 70E PPE Categories define the minimum arc-rated equipment required based on the calculated incident energy.

The category number on an arc flash label tells you the minimum category of PPE required — or the label may list the incident energy directly in cal/cm², in which case your PPE must be rated at or above that number.
Arc Thermal Performance Value (ATPV): The rating on arc-rated clothing — the "4 cal/cm²" or "8 cal/cm²" number on the tag — is called the ATPV (Arc Thermal Performance Value).
It represents the incident energy level at which the garment has a 50% probability of preventing a second-degree burn.
This means your PPE rating must always meet or exceed the incident energy shown on the label — never fall below it.
A 4 cal/cm² shirt does not protect you from a 6 cal/cm² arc flash.
Understanding Arc Flash Incident Energy — e-Hazard.
e-Hazard is a recognized leader in NFPA 70E training.
Their resource library covers how incident energy is calculated using IEEE 1584, how PPE ratings are selected, and how the numbers on your arc flash label were derived.
